source.title: Chevron Richmond Refinery Pipe Rupture and Fire - Final Investigation Report

actions_taken: Incident Command structure established with 20x20 ft hot zone
Feed to 4-sidecut line reduced by 5,000 bpd
Scaffolding erected to access leak location
Insulation removal attempted using pike pole and firefighters on scaffold
Fire hoses deployed and fire monitors positioned
Unit shutdown initiated after leak worsened
Community Warning System Level 3 alert issued
Shelter-in-place advisory issued for Richmond, San Pablo, and North Richmond
Post-incident 100 percent component inspection of all Crude Unit piping susceptible to sulfidation corrosion performed
Chevron developed and implemented new Leak Response Protocol post-incident
event.summary: On August 6, 2012, the Chevron Richmond Refinery #4 Crude Unit experienced a catastrophic pipe rupture on a 52-inch component of the 8-inch 4-sidecut line carrying light gas oil at approximately 640°F and 55 psig. The rupture resulted from severe wall thinning caused by sulfidation corrosion in low-silicon (0.01 wt%) ASTM A53B carbon steel. The released fluid vaporized into a large cloud that engulfed 19 employees. Ignition occurred approximately two minutes after the rupture. Eighteen employees escaped; one firefighter inside a fire engine survived due to full protective equipment. Six employees suffered minor injuries. Approximately 15,000 community members sought medical treatment. The incident resulted from a decade-long failure to implement internal recommendations for 100% component inspection or material upgrade, inadequate turnaround planning processes, poor emergency response, and safety culture deficiencies including reluctance to use Stop Work Authority.
recommendations: Revise API RP 939-C to establish minimum requirements for preventing catastrophic rupture of low-silicon carbon steel piping including mandatory 100% component inspection or replacement
Revise API RP 571 to better describe sulfidation corrosion characteristics in low-silicon carbon steel
Revise API 570 to align terminology, specify localized corrosion potential, and require leak response protocol compliance
Revise API RP 578 to require programs identifying low-silicon carbon steel piping circuits susceptible to sulfidation corrosion
Revise API RP 574 to incorporate API RP 939-C as normative reference and require leak response protocol compliance
Revise API RP 2001 to require facility-specific process fluid leak response protocols
Revise ASME PCC-2-2011 to require compliance with API RP 2001 leak response requirements before conducting leak repair
Chevron to develop accountability method for tracking implementation of ETC recommendations and industry best practices
Chevron to develop auditable process for denied or deferred turnaround work items related to mechanical integrity
Chevron to develop formal multi-person approval process prior to resetting minimum alert thickness values
Contra Costa County to revise ISO to require process safety culture continuous improvement program with oversight committee
City of Richmond to revise RISO to require process safety culture continuous improvement program with oversight committee

hazards.name: High-temperature flammable light gas oil under pressure in carbon steel piping susceptible to sulfidation corrosion
Low-silicon carbon steel piping with accelerated and variable sulfidation corrosion rates

threats.name: Sulfidation corrosion-induced wall thinning in low-silicon carbon steel piping component
Failure to perform 100% component inspection of susceptible piping
Inadequate turnaround planning process rejecting inspection and replacement recommendations
Aggressive on-stream leak mitigation activities worsening the leak

consequences.name: Catastrophic pipe rupture and large-scale loss of containment of flammable light gas oil
Vapor cloud formation and ignition causing fireball and fire
Worker injuries and near-fatalities (19 engulfed in vapor cloud, 6 minor injuries)
Community health impact - approximately 15,000 people sought medical treatment
Destruction of fire engine and property damage
Community Warning System Level 3 alert and shelter-in-place advisory

controls.name: Condition Monitoring Locations (CML) pipe wall thickness inspection program
controls.barrier_role: Detect wall thinning from sulfidation corrosion before failure
controls.lod_basis: Periodic inspection at designated CML points to monitor corrosion rates and remaining life

supporting_text: The 8-inch 4-sidecut piping had a total of 19 CMLs on piping and fittings. Historically, most of the CMLs measured corrosion rates at high silicon pipe-fitting components, such as elbow components.
no CMLs were placed on the low silicon piping component that failed
the 52-inch component in which the inspector identified corrosion in 2002—the component that failed on August 6, 2012—was never inspected again
This inspection data gathered using the 19 CMLs did not reflect the corrosion rates of the quickly corroding, low-silicon components of the 4-sidecut piping.

controls.name: 100% Component Inspection of sulfidation-susceptible carbon steel piping
controls.barrier_role: Identify all low-silicon components with accelerated corrosion rates before failure
controls.lod_basis: ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative requirement to inspect every component in high-risk circuits

supporting_text: the 4-sidecut piping was not 100 percent component inspected prior to the August 2012 incident
Chevron failed to perform internally recommended 100 percent component inspections

neither 100 percent component inspection of high-risk carbon steel piping nor upgrading susceptible piping to an inherently safer material of construction was fully performed at the Chevron Richmond Refinery

controls.name: Material of construction upgrade to higher chromium steel (9-Chrome)
controls.barrier_role: Eliminate hazard of variable sulfidation corrosion rates by substituting inherently safer material
controls.lod_basis: Inherently safer design - 9-Chrome steel corrodes approximately 9 times slower than carbon steel and eliminates silicon-based corrosion rate variability

supporting_text: recommendations to replace the 8-inch 4-sidecut piping during the 2007 and 2011 turnarounds were denied in accordance with the Framing Document criteria

Installation of 9-Chrome piping during turnaround opportunities prior to the August 6, 2012, incident to replace the 4-sidecut carbon steel would have both eliminated the hazard of silicon-based variable corrosion rates

the Reliability group made attempts to forego the burdensome inspection initiative and simply implement the ETC alternative sulfidation mitigation strategy to improve sulfidation corrosion resistance through using higher chromium steel by recommending replacement of the 8-inch 4-sidecut piping with inherently safer higher chromium steel. However, these recommendations were denied on multiple occasions

controls.name: Turnaround planning and work scope approval process (IMPACT/Framing Document)
controls.barrier_role: Ensure safety-critical inspection and replacement work is included in scheduled turnarounds
controls.lod_basis: Structured turnaround management process to identify and approve necessary maintenance and inspection work

supporting_text: The CSB found that the Richmond refinery's turnaround planning group rejected the recommendations to 100 percent component inspect or replace the portion of the 4-sidecut piping that ultimately failed

The Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative developed by the ETC metallurgist experts was not considered a valid mandate for justifying turnaround work which otherwise fell outside the acceptance criteria of the Framing Document

The current Chevron Richmond Refinery turnaround planning framework denies potential, discretionary turnaround work that does not yet have hard data gathered from refinery equipment to support it, even if the work request is based upon guidance issued by the industry trade association

controls.name: ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative and corporate technical guidance
controls.barrier_role: Communicate sulfidation corrosion risks and required inspection strategies to refinery personnel
controls.lod_basis: Corporate expert group recommendations and training to prevent sulfidation corrosion failures

supporting_text: Chevron ETC metallurgists released within Chevron a formal report dated September 30, 2009, nearly three years before the incident, titled Updated Inspection Strategies for Preventing Sulfidation Corrosion Failures in Chevron Refineries

Despite many attempts by Chevron ETC to warn and educate refinery personnel, neither 100 percent component inspection of high-risk carbon steel piping nor upgrading susceptible piping to an inherently safer material of construction was fully performed at the Chevron Richmond Refinery
No formal system is in place at the Chevron Richmond Refinery to communicate to the refinery management and to track to completion ETC findings and recommendations

controls.name: Minimum pipe thickness program and remaining life assessment
controls.barrier_role: Trigger replacement of piping before it reaches critically thin wall thickness
controls.lod_basis: Minimum Alert Thickness and Minimum Required Thickness calculations to determine when piping must be replaced

supporting_text: A structural minimum thickness value of 0.036-inch had been calculated for a small piping component within the 4-sidecut piping earlier during the turnaround. This 0.036-inch value was applied to the full length of the 8-inch 4-sidecut piping circuit.
Chevron does not require a formal multi-person review process to be performed prior to changing minimum thickness values and remaining life predictions.

A communication breakdown occurred in reviewing these results. The design engineer understood 'suction piping' to refer to only a small section of piping upstream of the 4-sidecut pump P-1149, while the inspector understood 'suction piping' to refer to the entire 4-sidecut circuit upstream of pump P-1149. They never met to clarify the calculation results.

controls.name: Unit Reliability Improvement Process (URIP) and Reliability Steering Committee
controls.barrier_role: Steward mechanical reliability and ensure implementation of safety-critical programs
controls.lod_basis: Formal reliability management process including monthly URBs and RSC meetings to track and implement reliability improvements

supporting_text: the URB meetings were not successful in effectively advocating for the ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative. The action items developed in the URB meetings were not implemented, and high-risk piping susceptible to sulfidation corrosion was not properly inspected or replaced

No additional implementation efforts or appeals to refinery management were attempted, and no high-level refinery managers who attended URBs and Reliability Steering Committee meetings took responsibility of the ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative

controls.name: Fixed Equipment Reliability Business Improvement Network (FER BIN)
controls.barrier_role: Promote and monitor implementation of new industry best practices and reliability initiatives across all Chevron refineries
controls.lod_basis: Corporate-wide expert network to align reliability practices and implement new standards

supporting_text: The FER BIN program did not effectively gain commitment from refinery management—the individuals capable of ensuring that the necessary reliability work was being performed—to implement the ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative

In March 2012, five months prior to the incident, the FER BIN Leader visited the Chevron Richmond refinery and identified that inspection of all carbon steel components susceptible to sulfidation corrosion was not being performed as recommended by the ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative
A replacement for the FER BIN Leader position was not assigned until four months after the previous FER BIN Leader's retirement

controls.name: Process Safety Indicators tracking program (OERI dashboard)
controls.barrier_role: Track leading and lagging process safety indicators to identify gaps before incidents occur
controls.lod_basis: Online dashboard tracking 26 process safety indicators reviewed weekly by management

supporting_text: Chevron does not track in OERI the implementation status of ETC recommendations or new industry guidance. Such an indicator could have ensured that the status of the ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative at the Chevron Richmond Refinery received greater management attention.
Chevron uses an online dashboard which was developed in 2009 called Operational Excellence and Reliability Intelligence (OERI) to track 26 different process safety indicators.

controls.name: Incident Command structure and hazardous area assessment during leak response
controls.barrier_role: Control emergency response, establish safe zones, and limit personnel exposure during leak event
controls.lod_basis: OSHA HAZWOPER requirements for Incident Command structure and hazard identification during emergency response

supporting_text: the appropriate technical expertise necessary to identify the potential for low-silicon, more rapidly corroding piping components in the 4-sidecut piping was not effectively consulted in the Incident Command structure on August 6, 2012
A hot zone of 20 feet by 20 feet was established and taped off around the leak location by the Incident Commander. The area outside of the hot zone was considered the cold zone, or safe zone.
When the 4-sidecut pipe ruptured, a very large vapor cloud formed which engulfed all personnel both within the hot zone and standing in what was considered the 'cold zone'

controls.name: Process leak response protocol
controls.barrier_role: Provide structured decision-making guidance for safe response to process fluid leaks including shutdown criteria
controls.lod_basis: Pre-established procedure requiring consultation with subject matter experts and defining shutdown criteria before mitigation attempts

supporting_text: On the day of the incident, Chevron had no leak response guidance or formal protocol for operations personnel, refinery management, emergency responders, or the Incident Commander to refer to when determining how to handle a process leak.
Following this incident, Chevron improved its internal policies by developing and implementing a leak response protocol for determining how to assess and mitigate leaks within the refinery.
The new leak response protocol would require unit shutdown if a similar leak were to occur in a Chevron refinery.

controls.name: Stop Work Authority (SWA) program
controls.barrier_role: Empower any individual to halt unsafe operations during emergency response
controls.lod_basis: Corporate policy granting authority to any individual to stop work when unsafe condition or act could result in undesirable event

supporting_text: Stop Work Authority was not used to require immediate and safe shutdown of the Crude Unit.
Some individuals even recommended that the Crude Unit be shut down, but they left the final decision to the management personnel present. No one formally invoked their Stop Work Authority.
Between 2008 and 2010, a statistically significant, increased portion of operators and mechanics began to feel that they could get in trouble when using their Stop Work Authority

controls.name: Emergency unit shutdown
controls.barrier_role: Stop flow of flammable process fluid to leaking pipe to prevent escalation
controls.lod_basis: Operational procedure to shut down crude unit to stop process fluid flow to leaking piping

supporting_text: The piping rupture and subsequent hydrocarbon release occurred two hours after the original leak was identified, which would have been enough time to execute an emergency shutdown of the Crude Unit
Several operations managers present then decided to shut the unit down, an action that requires hours to complete.
had Chevron decided to shut down the unit once staff knew the line could not be isolated, the pipe rupture and the endangerment of the community and Chevron personnel could have been avoided

controls.name: Firefighting water hoses and fire monitors
controls.barrier_role: Suppress fire and disperse vapor cloud during leak response
controls.lod_basis: Deployed fire hoses and monitors to respond to fire and vapor cloud during leak response

supporting_text: fire monitors were set up pointing towards the leak location, and two hose lines were run from the fire engine to a position near the 4-sidecut piping. Two teams of three firefighters operated the hoses. All hoses and monitors were at-the-ready
The hose teams immediately put out the fire, and both firefighters quickly came down from the scaffold
The hose teams attempted to keep the cloud at bay by spraying it with firefighting water.

controls.name: Community Warning System (CWS) and shelter-in-place advisory
controls.barrier_role: Warn community and reduce public exposure to released hazardous materials
controls.lod_basis: Contra Costa County Community Warning System Level 3 alert and shelter-in-place advisory for surrounding communities

supporting_text: a Community Warning System (CWS) Level 3 alert, and a shelter-in-place advisory (SIP) was issued at 6:38 p.m. for the cities of Richmond, San Pablo, and North Richmond

some phone calls notifying residents of the shelter-in-place advisory did not occur until over four hours after the release. This delay could have resulted in nearby residents unnecessarily and unknowingly being exposed to materials released to the atmosphere

controls.name: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) - full body firefighting protective equipment
controls.barrier_role: Protect individual firefighters from fire and heat exposure during incident
controls.lod_basis: Full-body fire-fighting protective equipment worn by Chevron Fire Department personnel

supporting_text: Because he was wearing full-body fire-fighting protective equipment, he was able to make his way through the flames to safety without physical injury.
He was able to escape through the flames surrounding the fire truck and make his way to safety without physical injury.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) including damage mechanism hazard review
controls.barrier_role: Identify sulfidation corrosion hazards and ensure adequate safeguards are in place
controls.lod_basis: Systematic hazard evaluation required by PSM regulations to identify process damage mechanisms and consequences of failure

supporting_text: Chevron ETC was a separate business entity within the Chevron corporation and had no direct authority over the reliability management within the Chevron refineries, Chevron ETC sulfidation corrosion experts had limited influence on what actually occurred within refineries in their areas of expertise. These individuals did not participate in refinery-specific processes such as Process Hazard Analyses (PHAs)

engage a diverse team of qualified personnel to perform a documented damage mechanism hazard review that identifies potential process damage mechanisms and consequences of failure and ensures safeguards are in place

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_references.PIF-001: Inspector competence gap - CML placed on high-silicon elbow adjacent to failed low-silicon component; 2002 inspection finding not entered as formal CML
pif_references.PIF-002: Communication breakdown between design engineer and inspector regarding scope of minimum thickness calculation for suction piping
pif_references.PIF-003: Organisational management failure - no high-level manager assigned accountability for ETC Sulfidation Failure Prevention Initiative implementation; bottom-up approach relying on lower-level employees without decision-making authority
pif_references.PIF-004: Training provided by ETC but not translated into action due to lack of management authority and accountability structures
pif_references.PIF-005: Situational awareness failure during emergency response - firefighters not informed of actual operating temperature (640°F vs believed 130°F); potential for pipe rupture not communicated to Incident Commander
pif_references.PIF-006: Procedures absent - no formal leak response protocol existed at time of incident; decisions made ad hoc under emergency pressure
pif_references.PIF-007: Safety culture - reluctance to use Stop Work Authority due to fear of reprisal, group-think, and deference to management hierarchy
